data.description:

While there has not been a single headline event driving the moves, ongoing operational updates and regional telecom developments have kept sentiment shifting over time.

Approach 1: Liberty Latin America Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today, so you can compare that value to the current share price.

For Liberty Latin America, the latest reported Free Cash Flow is about $197.3 million. Analysts provide explicit forecasts for the next few years, and Simply Wall St then extends those out using its own assumptions, giving projected Free Cash Flow of $707.5 million in 2030. All figures here are in $ and use a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, which models one period of higher growth followed by a more mature phase.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back and summed, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $34.58 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $7.73, this implies the stock is 77.6% undervalued based on this DCF framework.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Liberty Latin America Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings can be uneven, the P/S multiple is often a useful cross check because it focuses on how much investors are paying for each dollar of revenue rather than profit. It is especially helpful for telecom businesses, where accounting items can make net income less straightforward.

What counts as a "normal" or "fair" P/S depends on factors like expected growth in revenue, business risk and how stable margins are. Higher growth and lower risk usually line up with a higher justified multiple, while slower growth or higher uncertainty tend to support a lower one.

Liberty Latin America currently trades on a P/S of 0.35x. That sits below the Telecom industry average of 1.27x and below the peer average of 1.95x. data.title: Vertex Casgevy Rollout Tests Gene Editing Role In Vertex Earnings Story
data.description:
• Vertex Pharmaceuticals (NasdaqGS:VRTX) and CRISPR Therapeutics are ramping up patient treatments with Casgevy, a gene-editing therapy.
  • Casgevy is approved for sickle cell disease and beta thalassemia, marking a key step in bringing gene-editing into routine clinical use.
  • The current focus is on moving from approval to broader real-world deployment across eligible patient populations.

For you as an investor, Casgevy sits at the intersection of Vertex's established role in serious diseases and the growing use of gene-editing tools in medicine. While Vertex is already known for its work in other therapeutic areas, Casgevy adds exposure to a treatment approach that targets the underlying genetic drivers of disease rather than only managing symptoms.

The scale and pace of Casgevy uptake could influence how investors think about NasdaqGS:VRTX and gene-editing companies more broadly over time. As more patients start treatment, the focus is likely to stay on how smoothly the real-world rollout proceeds, patient access, and how payers respond to this kind of high-complexity therapy.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Vertex Pharmaceuticals by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Vertex Pharmaceuticals.

📰 Beyond the headline: 0 risks and 3 things going right for Vertex Pharmaceuticals that every investor should see.

For Vertex, scaling up Casgevy treatments with CRISPR Therapeutics looks like a concrete step in shifting from a cystic fibrosis focused story to one where gene-editing and non CF products play a bigger role. Management is already pointing to at least US$500 million in 2026 revenue from non CF products, with Casgevy specifically called out alongside JOURNAVX. That sits next to full year 2025 revenue of US$12.0b and a move from a US$535.6 million net loss to US$3.95b in net income, which shows the company now has meaningful earnings support for these newer launches.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Vertex Pharmaceuticals Narrative

  • Casgevy uptake adds weight to the narrative that Vertex can use its cystic fibrosis cash flows to build a broader precision medicine portfolio, including gene-editing for blood disorders.
  • If real world adoption of Casgevy or reimbursement terms are slower than Vertex anticipates, that could challenge expectations that new launches will materially expand earnings beyond the core cystic fibrosis franchise.
  • The specific revenue contribution from Casgevy within the US$500 million non CF guidance, and how that compares with ALYFTREK and JOURNAVX, is not fully detailed in the narrative and may become more important as reported figures break this out.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Vertex Pharmaceuticals to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Execution risk as Vertex and CRISPR Therapeutics move Casgevy from initial approvals into wider commercial use, including complex manufacturing and treatment center coordination.
  • ⚠️ Uncertainty around long term payer acceptance and pricing for high complexity gene-editing therapies, which could limit how large the addressable revenue pool becomes.
  • 🎁 Casgevy gives Vertex exposure to gene-editing alongside larger peers such as Pfizer and Regeneron that are also active in genetic medicines, potentially broadening future revenue beyond cystic fibrosis.
  • 🎁 Management is guiding to US$12.95b to US$13.1b of 2026 revenue with at least US$500 million from non CF products, showing Casgevy is already part of a more diversified earnings mix.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it makes sense to watch how quickly Vertex ramps patient infusions through its authorized treatment center network, how much of the non CF revenue bucket Casgevy represents as quarterly disclosures build up, and whether real world outcomes support broader use. You can also pay attention to how competitors in genetic medicine, such as CRISPR-focused peers and larger biopharma companies, respond with their own treatments and pricing. Any commentary from management at investor events on treatment volumes, reimbursement progress, and long term follow up data will be important signals for how central Casgevy could become in the Vertex story.

Approach 1: Getty Realty Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The DCF model here projects Getty Realty’s adjusted funds from operations into the future and then discounts those cash flows back to today, in order to estimate what the business could be worth in $ right now.

On this model, Getty Realty’s latest twelve month free cash flow is about $137.4 million. Using analyst inputs for the next few years, then extending those trends further out, Simply Wall St’s model projects free cash flow rising to about $258.6 million in 2035. All of these figures are in $ and use a two stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on adjusted funds from operations.

When those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $63.40 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $31.41, the model implies the stock is around 50.5% undervalued on this set of assumptions.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk usually point to a lower one.

At the same time, its electric fracking offerings target operators looking for high horsepower, grid or gas fueled operations at the wellsite. For you as an investor, this combination ties the company to both upstream activity and demand for reliable electricity for compute heavy facilities.

These moves indicate that NYSE:LBRT is aligning its service mix with long duration themes such as data center power needs and lower emission oilfield activity. As these power and electric fracking projects scale, the mix of earnings drivers could become more diversified compared with a purely completion services model. A central issue for investors is how quickly these newer lines can build meaningful contract volumes and recurring cash flows.

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a helpful way to see how much investors are paying for each euro of earnings. It links directly to what the business earns today, which many investors find easier to interpret than cash flow or asset based models.

What counts as a normal or fair P/E usually reflects two things: how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings appear. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher perceived risk often goes with a lower P/E.

Aegon currently trades on a P/E of 7.86x. That sits below the Insurance industry average of about 12.23x and below the peer group average of 13.05x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Aegon is 9.80x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E could be given factors such as Aegon’s earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market size and risk characteristics.

Compared with a simple peer or industry comparison, the Fair Ratio is designed to be more tailored, because it adjusts for growth, risk, profitability, sector and market cap rather than assuming one size fits all. On this basis, Aegon’s current P/E of 7.86x sits below the 9.80x Fair Ratio, which indicates that the shares may be undervalued on this metric.
